title: Getting Started
description: Your step-by-step guide to setting up a modern web development environment and creating your very first React project — no prior experience required.
keyPoints: Installed Node.js, VS Code, and Git — the core tools every web developer uses
Created a real React project using Next.js
Made your first code change and saw it update live in the browser
Learned how to start and stop the development server

blocks.heading: What You'll Need
blocks.body: Before we start installing anything, let's make sure you have the basics covered.

cards.title: A Computer
cards.body: Windows, Mac, or Linux — any will work. It does not need to be powerful. If it can run a web browser, it can run the tools we need.

cards.title: An Internet Connection
cards.body: You will need to download a few free programs and project files. After setup, most work can be done offline.

cards.title: Free Accounts (Optional)
cards.body: A GitHub account (github.com) will let you save your code online and collaborate with others. You can set this up later.

blocks.body: Everything we use in this guide is completely free. You do not need to buy any software, subscriptions, or courses.

blocks.title: Take Your Time
blocks.body: If you have never used a terminal before, some of these steps might feel unfamiliar. That is completely normal. Go at your own pace, and do not be afraid to re-read a step before moving on.

blocks.heading: Installing Your Tools
blocks.body: Choose your operating system below and follow the steps. Each one should take about 10-15 minutes.

blocks.title: Install Node.js
blocks.body:
Node.js lets your computer run JavaScript outside of a web browser. We need it to build React projects.

1. Open your web browser and go to nodejs.org.
2. Click the big green button that says LTS (Long Term Support). This downloads an installer file.
3. Open the downloaded file and click Next through each screen. Leave all the default options checked.
4. When it finishes, open the Command Prompt (press the Windows key, type "cmd", and hit Enter) and run the verification commands below.
If you see version numbers (like v20.11.0 and 10.2.0), Node.js is installed correctly.

blocks.title: Install VS Code
blocks.body:
Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free code editor. It is the most popular editor for web development and the one we will use throughout this guide.

1. Go to code.visualstudio.com and click Download for Windows.
2. Run the installer. On the "Select Additional Tasks" screen, check Add to PATH (it may already be checked).
3. Finish the installation and open VS Code.

blocks.title: Install Git
blocks.body:
Git is a version control tool. Think of it as an "undo history" for your entire project. You will also need it to collaborate with other developers later.

1. Go to git-scm.com and download the Windows installer.
2. Run the installer. The defaults are fine for every screen — just keep clicking Next.
3. Verify the installation:

blocks.title: Open the Terminal in VS Code
blocks.body:
The terminal is a text-based way to talk to your computer. VS Code has a built-in terminal so you never have to leave your editor.

Open VS Code and press Ctrl + ` (that is the backtick key, usually above the Tab key). A panel should appear at the bottom of the screen. This is your terminal.
From this point on, every command we ask you to run should be typed into this terminal inside VS Code.

blocks.title: Open Terminal
blocks.body: On a Mac you already have a built-in terminal. Press Cmd + Space to open Spotlight, type Terminal, and press Enter.

blocks.title: Install Homebrew
blocks.body:
Homebrew is a package manager for macOS. It lets you install developer tools with a single command instead of hunting for installers on the web.

Paste this entire line into your Terminal and press Enter:
The script will ask for your Mac password. When you type it, nothing will appear on screen — that is normal. Just type your password and press Enter.
After Homebrew finishes installing, it may print a message telling you to run two extra commands to add Homebrew to your PATH. Copy and run those commands exactly as shown.

blocks.title: Install Node.js and Git
blocks.body: Now that you have Homebrew, installing Node.js and Git is simple:

blocks.title: Install VS Code
blocks.body: You can install VS Code with Homebrew too:
After it finishes, you can open VS Code from your Applications folder or by typing `code .` in the terminal.

blocks.title: Open the Terminal Inside VS Code
blocks.body: Open VS Code and press Ctrl + ` (backtick key, above Tab). A terminal panel will appear at the bottom. Use this for the rest of the guide.
These instructions are for Ubuntu and Debian-based distributions. If you use a different Linux distribution, the package manager commands may differ, but the overall steps are the same.

blocks.title: Update Your System
blocks.body: Open a terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T) and make sure your package list is up to date:
blocks.code: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

blocks.title: Install Node.js, npm, and Git
blocks.body: Install everything with a single command:
blocks.code: sudo apt install -y nodejs npm git

blocks.body: The version of Node.js in the Ubuntu repositories can be outdated. If you need a newer version, visit nodejs.org and follow the instructions for installing via NodeSource.

blocks.title: Install VS Code
blocks.body: The easiest way on Ubuntu is with snap:
Alternatively, download the .deb package from code.visualstudio.com and install it with:

blocks.title: Open the Terminal Inside VS Code
blocks.body: Launch VS Code and press Ctrl + `. Use this built-in terminal for all the commands in this guide.
Quick-check: open Command Prompt or the VS Code terminal and run these three commands to confirm everything is ready.
If any command says 'is not recognized as an internal or external command', close your terminal and reopen it. Windows sometimes needs a fresh terminal to detect newly installed programs.
Quick-check: open Terminal or the VS Code terminal and run these commands to confirm everything is ready.
If 'code' is not found, open VS Code, press Cmd+Shift+P, type 'shell command', and select 'Install code command in PATH'.
Quick-check: open a terminal and run these commands to confirm everything is ready.
If 'node' returns a very old version (below 18), consider installing a newer version via NodeSource or using nvm (Node Version Manager).

blocks.heading: Creating Your First React Project
blocks.body: Time to build something real. We will use Next.js, a popular React framework that makes setting up a website easy.

blocks.title: Run the Create Command
blocks.body: In your VS Code terminal, type the following command and press Enter:

blocks.title: What is npx?
blocks.body: It is a tool that comes with Node.js. It lets you run packages without permanently installing them. Here it downloads the latest Next.js project creator and runs it for you.
If this is your first time, you may see the message: "Need to install the following packages — Ok to proceed? (y)". Type y and press Enter.

blocks.title: Answer the Setup Questions
blocks.body: The tool will ask you a series of questions. Here is what to choose for your first project:

rows: Would you like to use TypeScript?
Yes
Would you like to use ESLint?
Yes
Would you like to use Tailwind CSS?
Yes
Would you like your code inside a `src/` directory?
Yes
Would you like to use App Router?
Yes
Would you like to use Turbopack for next dev?
Yes
Would you like to customize the import alias?
No

blocks.body: Do not worry if you do not know what these options mean yet. We chose the most common defaults, and we will explain each one as we go through this guide.

blocks.title: Open Your Project and Start It
blocks.body: After the setup finishes, move into your new project folder and start the development server:
You should see output that includes something like:
Here is a more complete picture of what the terminal output looks like when the dev server starts successfully:

blocks.description: Full npm run dev output in the terminal
blocks.code:
$ npm run dev

> my-website@0.1.0 dev
> next dev --turbopack

  ▲ Next.js 15.x.x (Turbopack)
  - Local:        http://localhost:3000
  - Network:      http://192.168.1.42:3000
  - Environments: .env.local

 ✓ Starting...
 ✓ Ready in 1.8s

blocks.title: What Does "localhost" Mean?
blocks.body: localhost is your own computer acting as a web server. The :3000 part is the port number — think of it like a door number on a building. Your website is running behind door 3000 on your own machine. Nobody else on the internet can see it.

blocks.title: See Your Website in the Browser
blocks.body: Open your web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge — any will work) and go to:

blocks.title: Congratulations!
blocks.body: You are looking at a real React website running on your own computer. Every change you make to the code will show up here automatically.

blocks.heading: Understanding What You Just Created
blocks.body: Let us walk through the important files and folders in your new project. You do not need to memorize this, but knowing where things live will help you feel more confident.

blocks.heading: Project Structure
rows: package.json
The ID card of your project. It lists its name, the libraries it depends on, and the commands you can run (like npm run dev).
node_modules/
A folder full of code other people wrote that your project depends on. You never need to edit anything in here.
src/
Where YOUR code lives. Almost all of your work happens inside this folder.
src/app/
The App Router folder. Each subfolder inside here can become a page on your website.
src/app/layout.tsx
The root layout. It wraps every page on your site with shared elements like fonts and metadata.
src/app/page.tsx
The home page of your website. This is the file you see when you visit localhost:3000.
src/app/globals.css
Global CSS styles that apply to every page.
public/
Static files like images and icons. Anything you put here is accessible directly in the browser.
next.config.ts
Configuration for the Next.js framework. You rarely need to touch this as a beginner.
tsconfig.json
Configuration for TypeScript. The defaults are fine for now.
tailwind.config.ts
Configuration for Tailwind CSS, the styling library. We will explore this later.

blocks.heading: Making Your First Change
blocks.body: Nothing beats the thrill of changing code and watching the result appear instantly. Let us do that now.

blocks.title: Open the Home Page File
blocks.body: In VS Code, use the file explorer on the left side to navigate to:
Click on it to open it in the editor.

blocks.title: Quick Open Shortcut
blocks.body: You can also press Ctrl+P (Cmd+P on Mac) and type page.tsx to jump straight to the file. This shortcut becomes invaluable as your projects grow.

blocks.title: Find the Main Heading
blocks.body: Look for a line that contains text like "Get started by editing" or similar welcome text. It will be inside a <main> tag.

blocks.title: Replace It With Your Own Text
blocks.body: Delete the contents inside the <main> tag and replace them with something simple:

blocks.title: Save and Watch the Magic
blocks.body: Press Ctrl + S (or Cmd + S on Mac) to save the file. Now look at your browser — the page updates automatically without you having to refresh. This feature is called Hot Module Replacement and it makes development much faster.
You just wrote and deployed your first React code. Everything from here builds on exactly what you just did — editing files and watching the result in the browser.

blocks.heading: Try a Simple Component Modification
blocks.body: Now that you have the basics working, let us try something slightly more interesting. Replace the contents of your page.tsx with the following code to add a button that does something when clicked:

blocks.description: A simple interactive component with a button

blocks.title: What is "use client"?
blocks.body: Next.js components are server components by default. Adding "use client" at the top tells Next.js this component needs to run in the browser because it uses interactive features like useState. Do not worry about this distinction yet — we will cover it in detail later.

blocks.heading: Creating and Editing Files — A Walkthrough
blocks.body: As you build projects, you will constantly create new files and edit existing ones. Let us practice by adding a simple About page to your website.

blocks.title: Create a New Folder
blocks.body: In the VS Code file explorer on the left, right-click on the src/app folder and select New Folder. Name it about. In Next.js, each folder inside src/app becomes a route on your website, so this will create a page at localhost:3000/about.

blocks.title: Create the Page File
blocks.body: Right-click on the new about folder and select New File. Name it page.tsx. This is the file Next.js will render when someone visits /about.

blocks.title: Add Content to Your About Page
blocks.body: Type or paste the following code into the new file:

blocks.title: Visit Your New Page
blocks.body: Save the file (Ctrl+S / Cmd+S) and open your browser to http://localhost:3000/about. You should see your new About page. Click the "Go back home" link to return to the home page.

blocks.title: You Just Created a Multi-Page Website!
blocks.body: Every new folder with a page.tsx file inside src/app becomes a new page on your website. This is how Next.js routing works — no configuration needed.

blocks.heading: Stopping and Starting the Dev Server
blocks.body: The development server is the program that turns your code into a website you can view in the browser. Here is how to control it.

cards.title: Stopping the Server
cards.body: Go to the terminal in VS Code where the server is running and press Ctrl + C. This sends a "cancel" signal and stops the server. Your website will no longer be visible at localhost:3000 until you start it again.

cards.title: Starting the Server Again
cards.body: Make sure you are in the project folder, then run: npm run dev. If you closed the terminal and opened a new one, you may need to navigate back to the project folder first with: cd my-website && npm run dev

blocks.description: How stopping the dev server looks in the terminal

blocks.body: The ^C symbol you see in the terminal is the visual representation of pressing Ctrl+C. After that, the terminal returns to the normal command prompt ($), meaning the server has stopped and the terminal is ready for new commands.

blocks.title: Tip
blocks.body: You only need the server running while you are actively developing. It is perfectly fine to stop it when you are done for the day and start it again later. Your files are saved on your computer regardless of whether the server is running.

blocks.heading: Using Browser DevTools
blocks.body: Every modern browser has built-in developer tools that let you inspect your website, debug problems, and experiment with changes. Learning to use DevTools early will save you a lot of time.

blocks.code:
# Open DevTools in any browser:
# Press F12 on your keyboard
#   — or —
# Right-click anywhere on the page and select "Inspect"
#   — or —
# Use the keyboard shortcut:
#   Windows / Linux: Ctrl + Shift + I
#   Mac:             Cmd + Option + I

blocks.title: What Can DevTools Do?
blocks.body: The Elements tab shows the HTML structure of your page. The Console tab shows errors and lets you run JavaScript. The Network tab shows every file your page loads. For now, just get comfortable opening DevTools with F12 — we will use specific tabs in later chapters.

blocks.heading: Reading Error Messages
blocks.body: Errors are a normal part of coding. The key skill is not avoiding errors — it is reading them and knowing what to do. Let us look at a common example.
Suppose you accidentally have a typo in your component. For example, writing <mian> instead of <main>:

blocks.description: Example of code with a typo that causes a warning

blocks.body: This particular typo will not crash your app, but the browser console (DevTools > Console) may show a warning. Here is a more dramatic error — forgetting to close a JSX tag:

blocks.description: An error that Next.js shows in the browser and terminal
blocks.code:
x Expected closing tag for `

` ,-[src/app/page.tsx:4:1] 3 |
4 |

Hello! : ^^^^ 5 |

Welcome to my site.

: ^^ 6 |

`---- Caused by: Parsing ecmascript failed

blocks.title: How to Read an Error Message
blocks.body: Error messages follow a pattern: (1) what went wrong — e.g. 'Expected closing tag for <h1>', (2) where it happened — the file name and line number, and (3) a visual pointer showing the exact spot. Always start by reading the first line of the error, then look at the file and line number it mentions.

blocks.heading: Common Commands Reference
blocks.body: Here is a quick reference of the commands you will use most often as a beginner. Bookmark this section — you will come back to it.

rows: npm run dev
Starts the development server so you can view your site in the browser
Every time you begin working on your project
npm install
Downloads all the packages your project depends on (listed in package.json)
After cloning a project or when someone adds a new dependency
npm install <package>
Adds a new package (library) to your project
When you need new functionality, e.g. npm install axios
npx create-next-app@latest
Creates a brand-new Next.js project from scratch
Only when starting a new project
git status
Shows which files have been changed since your last save point (commit)
Before committing, to review what changed
git add .
Stages all changed files so they are ready to be committed
After making changes you want to save
git commit -m "message"
Saves a snapshot of your staged changes with a description
When you reach a good stopping point
Ctrl + C
Stops the currently running process in the terminal
When you need to stop the dev server or any stuck command
cd folder-name
Changes your terminal's current directory to the specified folder
To navigate into your project folder before running commands
ls (or dir on Windows)
Lists files and folders in the current directory
To see what is in the current folder

blocks.title: Tip: Use the Up Arrow Key
blocks.body: Press the Up arrow key in the terminal to cycle through your previous commands. This saves you from retyping npm run dev every time you restart the server.

blocks.heading: Common First-Day Problems
blocks.body: Almost everyone runs into at least one of these issues on their first day. Do not panic — they all have simple fixes.

items.title: Port 3000 is already in use
items.body:
This means another program (or another dev server you forgot to stop) is already using port 3000. You have two options:

1. Find and stop the other process. On Mac/Linux run: lsof -i :3000 then kill the process. On Windows run: netstat -ano | findstr :3000 then use Task Manager to end it.

2. Start your dev server on a different port instead:

npm run dev -- --port 3001

Then visit http://localhost:3001 in your browser.

items.title: 'node' is not recognized / command not found
items.body:
This means Node.js is not installed, or your terminal cannot find it. Try these steps:

1. Close your terminal completely and reopen it. Newly installed programs sometimes are not detected until you open a fresh terminal.

2. If that does not work, reinstall Node.js from nodejs.org and make sure to check the option to add it to your PATH during installation.

3. On Mac, if you installed via Homebrew, you may need to run the PATH commands that Homebrew printed after installation.

items.title: Permission errors (EACCES or EPERM)
items.body:
This usually happens on Mac or Linux when you try to install packages globally without the right permissions. Never use 'sudo npm install' for project dependencies — that creates files owned by root and causes more problems.

Instead:
- For global packages, configure npm to use a directory you own: mkdir ~/.npm-global && npm config set prefix '~/.npm-global' and add ~/.npm-global/bin to your PATH.
- For project-level installs, make sure you own the project folder: chown -R $(whoami) my-website/

items.title: 'npm run dev' starts but the page is blank or shows an error
items.body:
First, check the terminal for error messages — they usually tell you exactly what is wrong and on which line.

Common causes:
- A syntax error in your code (missing bracket, unclosed tag). Check the line number in the error message.
- You are editing the wrong file. Make sure you are editing src/app/page.tsx, not a file in node_modules.
- The browser is caching an old version. Try a hard refresh: Ctrl+Shift+R (Cmd+Shift+R on Mac).

If nothing works, you can always start fresh: delete the my-website folder and run npx create-next-app@latest my-website again.

items.title: 'npx create-next-app' hangs or is very slow
items.body:
This command downloads files from the internet, so it depends on your connection speed. On slow connections it can take several minutes. If it seems truly stuck:

1. Press Ctrl+C to cancel it.
2. Delete the partially created folder: rm -rf my-website (or delete it in your file explorer).
3. Try again. Sometimes the npm registry is slow temporarily.
4. If you are behind a corporate firewall or VPN, try disconnecting from the VPN temporarily.

blocks.title: When in Doubt, Read the Error Message
blocks.body: It is tempting to immediately search the internet when something breaks. But 90% of the time, the error message itself tells you exactly what went wrong and where. Always read the full error message first before searching for help.

blocks.heading: You Did It!
blocks.body: You installed all the tools a professional web developer uses, created a real React project, made your first code change, and learned how to control the development server. You are ready to move on to the next chapter.
